remarks.name: Dataiku Blog
remarks.role: AI governance, enterprise AI platform deployment, MLOps, analytics, agent orchestration.
remarks.text: Dataiku named a Gartner Magic Quadrant Leader for 5th consecutive year
remarks.content: For the fifth consecutive year, Dataiku has been named a Leader in the 2026 Gartner® Magic Quadrant™ for AI Platforms for Data Science and Machine Learning, once again recognized in the Leader quadrant for both “Completeness of Vision” and “Ability to Execute.”
remarks.contentTranslation: Dataiku连续第五年被评为2026年Gartner ®数据科学和机器学习AI平台魔力象限™的领导者，再次在“愿景的完整性”和“执行能力”的领导者象限中获得认可。
remarks.translation: Dataiku连续第五年被评为Gartner魔力象限领导者
remarks.observation: 这条言论适合观察 builder 对产品、创业、销售或市场节奏的判断；可作为选题线索，不直接等同于行业结论。

remarks.name: Lilian Weng's Blog (OpenAI)
remarks.role: AI research deep-dives, LLM architectures, agent systems, multimodal, alignment, safety.
remarks.text: Scaling Laws, Carefully
remarks.content: Scaling laws are one of the most critical empirical findings in deep learning. The observation is simple in form: the training loss $L$ decreases predictably as we scale up model size $N$, dataset size $D$, and compute $C$, following a power-law curve, which appears as a straight line on a log-log p
remarks.contentTranslation: 缩放定律是深度学习中最关键的经验发现之一。观察结果的形式很简单：当我们按照幂律曲线（在对数对数p上显示为直线）按比例放大模型大小$ N $、数据集大小$ D $和计算$ C $时，训练损失$ L $可预测地减少
remarks.translation: 谨慎地扩展法律
remarks.observation: 这条言论适合观察 builder 对产品、创业、销售或市场节奏的判断；可作为选题线索，不直接等同于行业结论。

remarks.name: Dataiku Blog
remarks.role: AI governance, enterprise AI platform deployment, MLOps, analytics, agent orchestration.
remarks.text: AI explainability in finance: auditable models, GenAI, and agents
remarks.content: Regulators are asking financial institutions a direct question: Can you explain how your AI made that decision? For most, the honest answer is no — not fully, not on demand, and not across every system that now influences regulated decisions. Compliance functions are expected to cover
remarks.contentTranslation: 监管机构正在向金融机构提出一个直接的问题：你能解释一下你的AI是如何做出这个决定的吗？对于大多数人来说，诚实的答案是否定的—不是完全的，不是按需提供的，也不是每个现在影响受监管决策的系统。合规职能预计将涵盖
remarks.translation: 财务中的AI可解释性：可审计模型、GenAI和Agent商
remarks.observation: 这条言论可作为 Agent 产品形态、协作方式或能力边界的观察入口；只代表公开观点，不作为公司事实主证据。

remarks.role: AI governance, enterprise AI platform deployment, MLOps, analytics, agent orchestration.
remarks.text: The AI success gap: why more AI doesn’t add up to more value
remarks.content: If AI is everywhere, why are so few companies getting real value from it?That is the question most enterprises are now facing. Eighty-eight percent of companies use AI, but only six percent generate meaningful value from it, according to a study from McKinsey.Pilots run, demos impress, a
remarks.contentTranslation: 如果AI无处不在，为什么很少有公司从中获得真正的价值？这是大多数企业现在面临的问题。根据麦肯锡的一项研究，百分之八十八的公司使用AI，但只有百分之六的公司从中产生有意义的价值。
remarks.translation: AI成功差距：为什么更多的AI不能带来更多的价值

remarks.name: Aaron Levie
remarks.role: ceo box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI
remarks.text:
AI regulation is far less simple than it looks. It’s prisoners dilemma at insane scale. 

In theory if all leading AI labs globally agreed to the same process of review and slow down, then we’d get frontier intelligence at similar rates and it diffuses relatively evenly. 

If the US remains at the frontier at all times, and has heavy regulation on the release of intelligence then we end up with an economic and geopolitical edge because we can control who has access to frontier intelligence. 

If we delay model releases, however, and another player - specifically China - doesn’t slow down and has equally strong models (not now but soon?) then our delays end up advantaging their models and eventually their tech stack.

Now, the US could ban these models, but that actually only puts the US at a steeper disadvantage because other countries won’t have those bans. Then, from a relative competitiveness standpoint the US has now fallen behind even though it started in front.

So, none of this is as simple as it looks. At some point it’s a simple bet of can closed models remain at the frontier in perpetuity or is there a risk of any other player or market catching up or just not falling behind.
remarks.translation:
AI监管远没有看起来那么简单。这是疯狂规模的囚犯困境。从理论上讲，如果全球所有领先的AI实验室都同意相同的审查过程并放慢速度，那么我们将以类似的速度获得前沿智能，并且它会相对均匀地扩散。
如果美国始终处于前沿，并且对情报的释放有严格的监管，那么我们最终会拥有经济和地缘政治优势，因为我们可以控制谁可以获得前沿情报。然而，如果我们推迟模型的发布，而另一个参与者-特别是中国-并没有放缓，并且拥有同样强大的模型（不是现在，而是很快？ ） ，那么我们的延迟最终会占据他们的模型和最终的技术堆栈的优势。
现在，美国可以禁止这些模式，但这实际上只会使美国处于更严重的劣势，因为其他国家不会有这些禁令。然后，从相对竞争力的角度来看，美国现在已经落后了，尽管它从前面开始。所以，这一切都不像看起来那么简单。
在某种程度上，这是一个简单的赌注，即CAN封闭模型永远留在前沿，或者是否有任何其他参与者或市场追赶或不落后的风险。
remarks.observation: 这条言论适合观察模型运行、算力、存储、sandbox 或开发平台基础设施的变化；后续事实判断需回到原始公告或技术文档。

remarks.name: Aaron Levie
remarks.role: ceo box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI
remarks.text:
We now have de facto AI regulation. It’s not obvious why from here on out models that have certain levels of capability or are trained on certain compute sizes won’t have to be reviewed by the government before release. 

Realistically, as AI models became more and more powerful this was going to be inevitable (I think it’s too early, but here we are). So now it’s mostly just interesting to think about the implications and scenarios from here. A few would be:

* America gets to control who gets access to frontier intelligence and when. This generally works as long as we remain at the frontier at all times and don’t have a risk of being surpassed. At the moment we have a clear lead in frontier intelligence so this is a good bet, but lots of motivated parties would love to change that.

* This likely creates backlog of AI releases which means that we will see less rapid fire back and forth jumps in model progress. Bull/fine case is that we just get bigger step functions per release at a slower rate and we end up at the same point we would have. Bear case is those incremental smaller jumps were necessary for the continued flywheel of innovation. 

* Other countries likely have even more incentive to at least hedge their bets with sovereign AI strategies so aren’t dependent on access to US AI all times. Previously this was relatively moot because the alternative wasn’t good enough, but that could change out of necessity and what we’re seeing in China.

* Open weights obviously a big winner here as it becomes what likely sovereign AI gets built out on, and what (for now) can still be released to the market without the same controls. One interesting question would be how regulation eventually extends to open models, which would have its own set of long term consequences.

Anyway some big updates to everyone’s mental models of AI regulation as a result of the capabilities we’re now seeing in AI. Wild times.
remarks.translation:
我们现在有了事实上的AI监管。不清楚为什么从现在开始，具有一定能力水平或接受过某些计算规模培训的模型在发布之前不需要政府审查。实际上，随着AI模型变得越来越强大，这将是不可避免的（我认为这还为时过早，但我们已经到了）。所以现在想想这里的含义和场景就很有趣了。
其中一些可能是： *美国可以控制谁以及何时获得前沿情报。只要我们始终处于前沿，并且没有被超越的风险，这通常是有效的。目前，我们在前沿情报方面有明显的领先优势，因此这是一个不错的选择，但许多有动力的政党都希望改变这一点。
*这可能会造成AI发布的积压，这意味着我们将看到模型进度不那么快的来回跳跃。公牛/精细的情况是，我们只是以较慢的速度在每个版本中获得更大的步骤函数，并且我们最终会达到相同的点。熊壳是持续创新飞轮所必需的增量小跳跃。
*其他国家可能更有动力至少用主权AI策略对冲他们的赌注，因此并不总是依赖于对美国AI的访问。此前，这是相对没有实际意义的，因为替代方案还不够好，但这可能出于必要以及我们在中国看到的情况而发生变化。
*开放式权重显然是一个大赢家，因为它可能成为主权AI的基础，并且（目前）仍然可以在没有相同控制的情况下向市场发布。一个有趣的问题是，监管最终如何扩展到开放模型，这将产生一系列长期后果。无论如何，由于我们现在在AI中看到的功能，每个人的AI调节心理模型都发生了一些重大更新。野生时代。
remarks.observation: 这条言论适合观察模型运行、算力、存储、sandbox 或开发平台基础设施的变化；后续事实判断需回到原始公告或技术文档。

remarks.text:
This is a big misconception 

Somehow people think building an audience is antithetical to building a company. In fact, they’re increasing becoming the same thing

Storytelling (to users/customers, investors, candidates) is arguably the founder’s most important job

Building is getting cheaper, attention is getting more expensive
remarks.translation: 这是一个很大的误解。不知何故，人们认为建立受众群体与建立公司背道而驰。事实上，他们越来越多地成为讲故事（对用户/客户，投资者，候选人）是创始人最重要的工作建筑越来越便宜，注意力越来越昂贵

remarks.name: Aditya Agarwal
remarks.role: General Partner SouthPkCommons, Co-Founder Bevel_Health
remarks.text:
It is stupidly hard to start a pure software company right now.

Customers don't want software, they want outcomes. So you end up having to do a lot of custom work for them. 

Anything horizontal is on a ticking clock.

Every customer wants to deeply customize.

There are opportunities.

But you need to have a very heretical 2-3 year view of the world in order to build something good.
remarks.translation: 现在创办一家纯软件公司非常困难。客户不想要软件，他们想要结果。因此，您最终不得不为他们做大量的自定义工作。任何水平的东西都在滴答作响的时钟上。每个客户都希望深入定制。有机会。但你需要有一个非常异端的2-3年的世界观，才能建立一些好的东西。
remarks.observation: 这条言论适合观察 AI 编程工具、设计到代码流程和软件生产方式的变化；需要结合产品发布或文档再做事实判断。

podcasts.name: Unsupervised Learning (Redpoint)
podcasts.title: Ep 89: AI Research Legend’s Honest Assessment of Where We Are
podcasts.excerpt: This episode with Lukasz Kaiser, co-author of the seminal "Attention Is All You Need" transformer paper and former researcher at both Google Brain and OpenAI, is a wide-ranging conversation about the fundamental limits of current AI architectures and whether transformers will continue to dominate or eventually give way to something new. Lukasz brings a rare dual perspective: deep belief in how far the current paradigm has taken us (he's an enthusiastic daily Codex user who's seen 10x productivity gains in his own research), while maintaining genuine intellectual humility about whether transformers can truly generalize the way humans do. The episode weaves together questions about data efficiency, the non-verifiable RL frontier, the coding agent revolution, the open vs. closed source gap, and what the next architectural leap might look like: all filtered through the lens of someone who helped bu…

Lukasz brings a rare dual perspective: deep belief in how far the current paradigm has taken us (he's an enthusiastic daily Codex user who's seen 10x productivity gains in his own research), while maintaining genuine intellectual humility about whether transformers can truly generalize the way humans do. The episode weaves together questions about data efficiency, the non-verifiable RL frontier, the coding agent revolution, the open vs. closed source gap, and what the next architectural leap might look like: all filtered through the lens of someone who helped bu…

podcasts.name: Unsupervised Learning (Redpoint)
podcasts.title: Ep 88: Unpacking DeepMind's Quest for SuperIntelligence with Demis Hassabis' Biographer
podcasts.excerpt: Sebastian Mallaby spent three years and 30+ hours interviewing Demis Hassabis in the back of a British pub to write The Infinity Machine, and the conversation uses that reporting to surface the most underexplored figure in AI. Demis founded the original AI lab in 2010, won a Nobel Prize, runs models that consistently top the leaderboards, and yet remains so unrecognized that Sebastian's own publisher worried no one would buy a book with his face on the cover. The throughline is a paradox: Demis tried to prevent the AI race we're now all living through, and now finds himself one of its central protagonists. He used to believe a single lab could carry the safety burden to AGI; he now sees safety as a collective action problem only governments can solve. He hedged DeepMind's research bets across every promising direction, and as a result missed the two most consumer-defining moments…
